text: Frankly, my dear, I don't give a damn.
speaker: Gone with the Wind (1939)
context: Rhett Butler's (Clark Gable) parting words to Scarlett O'Hara as he leaves her at the end of the film

text: Here's looking at you, kid.
speaker: Casablanca (1942)
context: Rick Blaine (Humphrey Bogart) says this to Ilsa (Ingrid Bergman) as a recurring toast, most memorably at the airport farewell.

text: I'm gonna make him an offer he can't refuse.
speaker: The Godfather (1972)
context: Don Vito Corleone (Marlon Brando) tells his godson Johnny Fontane how he'll persuade movie producer Jack Woltz to cast him.

text: Toto, I've a feeling we're not in Kansas anymore.
speaker: The Wizard of Oz (1939)
context: Dorothy, having landed in the strange new world of Oz after the tornado, says this to her dog Toto.

text: You're gonna need a bigger boat.
speaker: Jaws (1975)
context: Chief Brody, after seeing the shark's full size while chumming, backs into the cabin and says this to Quint.

text: May the Force be with you.
speaker: Star Wars (1977)
context: Said during the Rebel briefing before the assault on the Death Star (and by Han to Luke).

text: I'll be back.
speaker: The Terminator (1984)
context: The Terminator (Arnold Schwarzenegger) tells the police desk sergeant he'll be back, then drives a car through the front of the station.

text: I feel the need... the need for speed!
speaker: Top Gun (1986)
context: Maverick (Tom Cruise) and Goose (Anthony Edwards) say it together, high-fiving as they get pumped up to fly.

text: You can't handle the truth!
speaker: A Few Good Men (1992)
context: Col. Nathan Jessep (Jack Nicholson) erupts on the witness stand during cross-examination by Lt. Daniel Kaffee (Tom Cruise).

text: My mama always said life was like a box of chocolates. You never know what you're gonna get.
speaker: Forrest Gump (1994)
context: Forrest (Tom Hanks) sits on a bus-stop bench recounting his mother's favorite saying.

text: Say 'what' again. I dare you, I double dare you.
speaker: Pulp Fiction (1994)
context: Jules (Samuel L. Jackson) menaces Brett, a terrified young man, during the apartment confrontation.

text: Life finds a way.
speaker: Jurassic Park (1993)
context: Dr. Ian Malcolm (Jeff Goldblum) argues that the park's all-female engineered dinosaurs cannot truly be prevented from breeding or controlled.

text: I'm the king of the world!
speaker: Titanic (1997)
context: Jack (Leonardo DiCaprio) shouts triumphantly from the bow of the ship, arms outstretched.

text: Here's Johnny!
speaker: The Shining (1980)
context: Jack Torrance (Jack Nicholson) hacks through a bathroom door with an axe and grins through the hole.

text: Why so serious?
speaker: The Dark Knight (2008)
context: The Joker (Heath Ledger) taunts a victim while telling a story about how he got his scars

text: One does not simply walk into Mordor.
speaker: The Lord of the Rings: The Fellowship of the Ring (2001)
context: Boromir (Sean Bean) arguing against the plan to destroy the Ring

text: I am Iron Man.
speaker: Iron Man (2008)
context: Tony Stark (Robert Downey Jr.) goes off-script and reveals his identity to the press at the very end of the film.

text: I'm kind of a big deal.
speaker: Anchorman: The Legend of Ron Burgundy (2004)
context: Ron Burgundy (Will Ferrell) boasting to Veronica Corningstone about his fame

text: I drink your milkshake! I drink it up!
speaker: There Will Be Blood (2007)
context: Daniel Plainview (Daniel Day-Lewis) berating Eli Sunday in the bowling-alley confrontation

text: A million dollars isn't cool. You know what's cool? A billion dollars.
speaker: The Social Network (2010)
context: Sean Parker (Justin Timberlake) pitching ambition to Mark Zuckerberg and Eduardo Saverin at a restaurant; the punchline lands in an intercut deposition scene

text: Did we just become best friends?
speaker: Step Brothers (2008)
context: Brennan (Will Ferrell) to Dale (John C. Reilly) after they bond over shared interests; Dale replies "Yup!"

text: On Wednesdays we wear pink.
speaker: Mean Girls (2004)
context: Karen Smith explaining the Plastics' dress code rule to Cady

text: The best way to spread Christmas cheer is singing loud for all to hear.
speaker: Elf (2003)
context: Buddy the Elf sharing his philosophy on holiday spirit

text: To infinity and beyond!
speaker: Toy Story (1995)
context: Buzz Lightyear's heroic catchphrase as he strikes a pose and 'flies'

text: Hakuna matata.
speaker: The Lion King (1994)
context: Timon and Pumbaa teach Simba their carefree "no worries" philosophy, both as spoken dialogue and as the title song.

text: Ogres are like onions.
speaker: Shrek (2001)
context: Shrek explains to Donkey that ogres have layers

text: Just keep swimming.
speaker: Finding Nemo (2003)
context: Dory's optimistic mantra, which she sings to Marlin to keep him going during the search for Nemo.

text: As you wish.
speaker: The Princess Bride (1987)
context: Westley the farmhand says this to Buttercup; it's revealed to be his way of saying "I love you," and recurs throughout the film.

text: Be the ball.
speaker: Caddyshack (1980)
context: Ty Webb (Chevy Chase) giving zen golf advice to caddy Danny Noonan during their night-golf lesson

text: Yo, Adrian!
speaker: Rocky (1976)
context: Rocky Balboa addressing Adrian; "Yo, Adrian! It's me, Rocky." The phrase appears in the 1976 film.

text: As far back as I can remember, I always wanted to be a gangster.
speaker: Goodfellas (1990)
context: Henry Hill's (Ray Liotta) opening voiceover narration

text: The first rule of Fight Club is: you do not talk about Fight Club.
speaker: Fight Club (1999)
context: Tyler Durden (Brad Pitt) laying down the rules to the members of Fight Club.

text: The Dude abides.
speaker: The Big Lebowski (1998)
context: The Dude (Jeff Bridges) to The Stranger (Sam Elliott) at the bar near the film's end, summing up his laid-back philosophy.

text: Are you not entertained?
speaker: Gladiator (2000)
context: Maximus (Russell Crowe) shouts at the Zucchabar arena crowd after killing his opponents in a brutal fight.

text: Houston, we have a problem.
speaker: Apollo 13 (1995)
context: Aboard the damaged spacecraft, the crew reports the malfunction to mission control. The line is spoken by Jim Lovell (Tom Hanks), echoing Jack Swigert; the film's "we have a problem" differs from the real transmission's "we've had a problem."

text: Death's at the bottom of everything, Martins. Leave death to the professionals.
speaker: The Third Man (1949)
context: Major Calloway (Trevor Howard) to pulp novelist Holly Martins (Joseph Cotten), warning him off his amateur investigation in postwar Vienna.

text: How could I have known that murder can sometimes smell like honeysuckle?
speaker: Double Indemnity (1944)
context: Insurance salesman Walter Neff (Fred MacMurray) narrating his dictaphone confession about the affair and murder plot with Phyllis Dietrichson.

text: The cheaper the crook, the gaudier the patter.
speaker: The Maltese Falcon (1941)
context: Private detective Sam Spade (Humphrey Bogart) sizing up the small-time gunman Wilmer.

text: I hate that word. It's a return, a return to the millions of people who have never forgiven me for deserting the screen.
speaker: Sunset Boulevard (1950)
context: Faded silent star Norma Desmond (Gloria Swanson) correcting screenwriter Joe Gillis (William Holden) when he calls her planned return a 'comeback.'

text: Never knew a prospector yet that died rich.
speaker: The Treasure of the Sierra Madre (1948)
context: Grizzled old prospector Howard (Walter Huston) offering hard-won wisdom about the gold-hunting life.

source.title: The Treasure of the Sierra Madre - Wikiquote

text: That's the way it crumbles, cookie-wise.
speaker: The Apartment (1960)
context: C.C. 'Bud' Baxter (Jack Lemmon) parroting his boss Sheldrake's glib corporate-speak philosophy back to him.

text: Funny business, a woman's career. The things you drop on your way up the ladder so you can move faster. You forget you'll need them again when you get back to being a woman.
speaker: All About Eve (1950)
context: Aging Broadway star Margo Channing (Bette Davis) reflecting on the costs of ambition.

text: I came to Casablanca for the waters.
speaker: Casablanca (1942)
context: Rick Blaine (Humphrey Bogart) deflecting Captain Renault's (Claude Rains) question about why he came to Casablanca; Renault replies there are no waters in the desert, and Rick says he was misinformed.

text: Is this testing whether I'm a Replicant or a lesbian, Mr. Deckard?
speaker: Blade Runner (1982)
context: Rachael (Sean Young) to Rick Deckard (Harrison Ford) during her Voight-Kampff test at the Tyrell Corporation, early in the film.

text: The world is a business, Mr. Beale. It has been since man crawled out of the slime.
speaker: Network (1976)
context: Corporate chairman Arthur Jensen (Ned Beatty) delivering his boardroom sermon to news anchor Howard Beale (Peter Finch).

text: You're an errand boy sent by grocery clerks to collect a bill.
speaker: Apocalypse Now (1979)
context: Colonel Kurtz (Marlon Brando) to Captain Willard (Martin Sheen) at his compound, dismissing Willard's mission.

text: Some places are like people. Some shine and some don't.
speaker: The Shining (1980)
context: Head chef Dick Hallorann (Scatman Crothers) explaining 'the shining' to young Danny Torrance (Danny Lloyd) at the Overlook Hotel.

text: Loneliness has followed me my whole life. Everywhere.
speaker: Taxi Driver (1976)
context: Travis Bickle (Robert De Niro) in voice-over narration from his diary.

text: Mr. Vaughn, what we are dealing with here is a perfect engine, an eating machine.
speaker: Jaws (1975)
context: Marine biologist Matt Hooper (Richard Dreyfuss) warning Mayor Vaughn (Murray Hamilton) about the shark.

text: I'm not sure that I agree with you a hundred percent on your policework there, Lou.
speaker: Fargo (1996)
context: Marge Gunderson (Frances McDormand), the pregnant police chief, gently correcting her deputy Lou's flawed reasoning at a crime scene.

text: Smokey, this is not 'Nam. This is bowling. There are rules.
speaker: The Big Lebowski (1998)
context: Walter Sobchak (John Goodman) pulling a gun over a foot-fault dispute during league bowling, to opponent Smokey.

text: Have you a valediction, boyo?
speaker: L.A. Confidential (1997)
context: Captain Dudley Smith (James Cromwell) to Jack Vincennes (Kevin Spacey), moments before shooting him.

text: It appears my hypocrisy knows no bounds.
speaker: Tombstone (1993)
context: Doc Holliday (Val Kilmer), the consumptive gambler-gunslinger, wryly acknowledging his own contradictions.

text: Rough business, this movie business. I may have to go back to loan sharking for a rest.
speaker: Get Shorty (1995)
context: Chili Palmer (John Travolta), the Miami loan shark turned Hollywood player, at the film's close.

text: Don't ever let yourself get attached to anything you are not willing to walk out on in 30 seconds flat if you feel the heat around the corner.
speaker: Heat (1995)
context: Master thief Neil McCauley (Robert De Niro) explaining his creed to detective Vincent Hanna across a diner table.

text: I specifically reminded her; bedside table. On the kangaroo.
speaker: Pulp Fiction (1994)
context: Boxer Butch Coolidge (Bruce Willis) fuming that his girlfriend Fabienne forgot his late father's gold watch.

text: You need to call it. I can't call it for you. It wouldn't be fair.
speaker: No Country for Old Men (2007)
context: Anton Chigurh (Javier Bardem) to the gas station proprietor during the coin-toss scene, pressing him to call heads or tails for his life.

source.title: No Country for Old Men (film) - Wikiquote

text: I have a competition in me. I want no one else to succeed. I hate most people.
speaker: There Will Be Blood (2007)
context: Daniel Plainview (Daniel Day-Lewis) confessing his misanthropy to the man claiming to be his half-brother Henry.

text: I'm not the guy you kill, I'm the guy you buy!
speaker: Michael Clayton (2007)
context: Michael Clayton (George Clooney) shouting at Karen Crowder near the film's climax, after realizing the conspiracy against him.

text: This town deserves a better class of criminal, and I'm gonna give it to 'em.
speaker: The Dark Knight (2008)
context: The Joker (Heath Ledger) addressing Gotham's mob bosses during his hostile takeover of their operation.

text: Each and every man under my command owes me one hundred Nazi scalps.
speaker: Inglourious Basterds (2009)
context: Lt. Aldo Raine (Brad Pitt) rallying his squad of Jewish-American soldiers in his opening address.

text: I guess we learned not to do it again.
speaker: Burn After Reading (2008)
context: The CIA Superior (J.K. Simmons) in the final debrief scene, summarizing the botched operation to Officer Palmer.

text: I don't want to be a product of my environment. I want my environment to be a product of me.
speaker: The Departed (2006)
context: Frank Costello (Jack Nicholson) in the film's opening voiceover, laying out his philosophy of power in South Boston.

text: There's something about this that's so black, it's like how much more black could this be? And the answer is none. None more black.
speaker: This Is Spinal Tap (1984)
context: Nigel Tufnel (Christopher Guest) shows off a new album cover; his deadpan musing on its blackness.

text: Back off, man. I'm a scientist.
speaker: Ghostbusters (1984)
context: Dr. Peter Venkman (Bill Murray) defends his research methods to a skeptical librarian.

text: Rudeness is merely an expression of fear. People fear they won't get what they want. The most dreadful and unattractive person only needs to be loved, and they will open up like a flower.
speaker: The Grand Budapest Hotel (2014)
context: M. Gustave (Ralph Fiennes), the fastidious concierge, instructs his lobby boy Zero on his philosophy of service.

text: Strange women lying in ponds distributing swords is no basis for a system of government.
speaker: Monty Python and the Holy Grail (1975)
context: Dennis the peasant (Michael Palin) argues political theory with King Arthur about the Lady of the Lake.

source.title: Monty Python and the Holy Grail - Wikiquote

text: This? Why, I could make a hat, or a brooch, or a pterodactyl.
speaker: Airplane! (1980)
context: Johnny (Stephen Stucker), the air traffic control clown, riffs when asked what he can make of the situation.

text: Looks like I picked the wrong week to quit smoking.
speaker: Airplane! (1980)
context: Steve McCroskey (Lloyd Bridges), the harried air traffic supervisor, laments amid the crisis (the first of his escalating vices).
